## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, this is part two of creating pre-launch content and I want to talk about screen cap video versus full motion video. This will be a big decision for a lot of people. Screen capture, which again is just basically, well you're looking at a screen capture right now, it's just a video that captures stuff going on on your computer screen while you talk over it. Screen capture is often a lot easier than full motion for lots of reasons. Basically, this video, alls I did was create a keynote or a PowerPoint presentation and then I turned out my recorder and I hooked up my microphone and I started talking. As opposed to if this was a full motion video, boy we'd have to put together some type of video shot. It might be the shot where I'm at my whiteboard, but we'd have to put together a shot and I have to get my camera man, we'd have to get the lights all hooked up. I'd have to care about how I looked. I'd have to get dressed or something maybe. There's just a lot more going on generally with a full motion whereas a quick and dirty screen cap can be a lot easier. It can be a lot easier to deliver a script with a screen cap video. This video is not scripted. We're going over material I know very well so I can just basically hit the record button and start talking and just deliver the content. If this was a pre-launch content, I wanted to be generally our pre-launch videos, our pre-launch content is a lot more tightly scripted. Even if it's just bullet points, I still have a pretty darn good idea of exactly how I want to go through it. If this is your first launch or you're just starting out, you probably want to script it pretty darn tightly. Even now with all the experience I have, my pre-launch videos are pretty darn scripted. They're not word for word, especially in the teaching portions. I just go from bullet points, but usually the beginning of the video and the end of the video, pretty darn scripted. Again, I'm usually not delivering line by line to the script. I've at least written it out and I've studied it quite a bit and I'm ready to go with the script. The thing is, if you're going to deliver from a script with a screen capture, it's a lot easier for you. You can be reading off a printed script. You can be reading off a script on your computer screen. With pre-launch videos, you will record your script. You'll actually record the audio first. There's no video going on. You'll record the audio first and then you'll do your screen capture to the audio recording.

## [03:02 - 06:07]

You're doing one piece at a time. That's just a lot easier than standing in front of a camera and delivering a scripted message. The screen caps, again, I just mentioned that. With screen caps, basically you record the audio first and then you do the on-screen video recording. That is if you're going to do a heavily scripted piece. Something like this, where I'm just speaking to my PowerPoints and I'm in a teaching mode. You don't need to do that, but if it's a piece of pre-launch content that's tightly scripted, then a lot of times you'll record the audio first. When you do that, it's a lot easier. You can just read it off your computer screen. You can read it off a printout. You can't do that so much with a full-motion video. Another thing about full-motion video is some people really don't like to be on camera. They find that full-motion is really painful. A lot of times people are like, oh, I'll start shooting full-motion when I lose 20 pounds or something like that. A lot of people just don't like how they look. They're concerned about what to do with their hands. They're concerned about what to do with their eyes. They're concerned about their dress. The reality is, for most of that stuff, you just don't worry about it that much and just start getting in front of the camera and practicing it pretty soon. It'll be pretty natural. But a lot of times people don't like to be on camera and if that's the case, then screen capture is the way to go. This is one that's in favor of full-motion. It's generally easier for the viewer to connect with you if it's a full-motion video. Like right now you can't see me. You can't see the expression on my face. You can't see any gestures I'm making. It's harder to connect with someone if it's a screen capture video. You've seen a lot of me in full-motion video and you've seen me both in my pre-launch and we'll use a lot of full-motion videos within this course. You've seen me so you've probably built up that connection. But if you had never seen me in full-motion, you just wouldn't have as close a connection with me as you do with full-motion. Generally it's a lot easier to edit screen capture after the fact. If I forget to say something here, I can literally go back and just record another audio piece and just insert it right into this video. You would never know the difference. It's by the nature of this, the editing process of screen capture. It's a lot easier to do that. If you shoot a full-motion video and then all of a sudden like a day or two from now, you realize, oh, I forgot about that one piece. It's harder to go and edit that piece in later after the fact. It's possible but you have to worry about all this, the set being perfectly the same, your clothes being perfectly the same, your hair being perfectly the same.

## [06:07 - 08:33]

It's just a lot harder to do something like that after the fact. In fact, if you find a situation where you do need to edit something into a full-motion video, a lot of times the best way to do it is to actually record it as a screen cap and just put a little screen cap section in the middle of your full-motion video. Now, it can be awkward if you've got like a 15-minute video and then you just have one piece of screen cap in the middle. So you have to play with that. You might have to add several different screen cap segments but you could do that. But the overall message is that it is easier to edit screen capture after the fact than it is to do the same with full-motion video. Screen capture can be a lot faster to create. This is this, I mean, obviously this video, I'm basically just turn on the recorder and I'm speaking into it. Now if it is a tightly scripted piece of pre-launch content, it can still be a lot easier, a lot faster to create than a full-motion video. Now on the other hand, screen capture can be slower to create if you want to use a whole bunch of fancy animations. In that case, if you want to have different pictures flying in and out, you can get very, very fancy with screen capture video. Once you start to do that, well, it does get to be slower. It's a lot more effort to create. Another thing that's important to remember is you can mix full-motion with screen capture video. So I have done this a lot, probably the last six or eight launches I've done something like this. For me, I often do this blueprint video at the end of my, the third piece of pre-launch content is traditionally. And what I'll do is I'll have a full-motion intro where I'm talking directly to the camera, talking about what the viewer is going to get. And then we'll cut to a full, to a screen capture video and I'll deliver a whole bunch of content via screen capture. And then often at the end, I'll come back and do a full-motion conclusion. And there's all different ways to do this. You can do something that's almost all full-motion with some screen cap pieces that come in. If you're talking about a certain bullet points, you know, but you can, you can get very creative. You can mix and match the two of them.
